content:
# Luke Chapter 20 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 113-114 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_020_page_001.jpg, LUK_020_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And it came-to-be on one of the days, [while] he [was] teaching the people in the temple and proclaiming-the-good-news, the chief-priests and the scribes stood-upon [him] with the elders,

**2.** and spoke, saying to him, "Tell us by what authority you do these [things], or who is the [one] having given you this authority?"

**3.** And having answered, he said to them, "I also will ask you a word, and tell me:

**4.** The baptism of John — was it from heaven, or from men?"

**5.** And they reasoned-together among themselves, saying, "If we should say, 'From heaven,' he will say, 'Why did you not believe him?'

**6.** But if we should say, 'From men,' all the people will stone us, for they are having been persuaded [that] John is a prophet."

**7.** And they answered [that they] did not know from-where [it was].

**8.** And Jesus said to them, "Neither do I tell you by what authority I do these [things]."

**9.** And he began to speak to the people this parable: "A man planted a vineyard, and let-it-out to tenant-farmers, and went-away-from-home [for] a considerable time.

**10.** And at [the proper] season he sent to the tenant-farmers a servant, so-that they would give him from the fruit of the vineyard; but the tenant-farmers, having beaten him, sent [him] away empty.

**11.** And he proceeded to send another servant; and that [one] also, having beaten and having dishonored, they sent [him] away empty.

**12.** And he proceeded to send a third; and this [one] also, having wounded, they cast-out.

**13.** And the lord of the vineyard said, 'What shall I do? I will send my beloved son; perhaps they will respect this [one].'

**14.** But having seen him, the tenant-farmers were reasoning with one-another, saying, 'This is the heir; let us kill him, so-that the inheritance might become ours.'

**15.** And having cast him outside the vineyard, they killed [him]. What therefore will the lord of the vineyard do to them?

**16.** He will come and will destroy these tenant-farmers, and will give the vineyard to others." And having heard [this], they said, "May-it-not-be!"

**17.** And he, having looked-at them, said, "What then is this [that] has been written: '[The] stone which the [ones] building rejected — this became [the] head of [the] corner'?

**18.** Everyone having fallen upon that stone will be broken-to-pieces; but upon whomever it falls, it will crush him."

**19.** And the scribes and the chief-priests sought to lay hands upon him in that [very] hour, and they feared the people; for they knew that he spoke this parable against them.

**20.** And having watched-closely, they sent-forth spies, pretending themselves to be righteous, so-that they might seize-upon a word of his, so-as to hand him over to the rule and to the authority of the governor.

**21.** And they questioned him, saying, "Teacher, we know that you speak and teach rightly, and you do not receive [the] face [of anyone], but upon [the basis of] truth you teach the way of God.

**22.** Is it lawful for us to give tribute to Caesar, or not?"

**23.** But having perceived their craftiness, he said to them,

**24.** "Show me a denarius. Whose image and inscription does it have?" And they said, "Caesar's."

**25.** And he said to them, "Therefore give-back the [things] of Caesar to Caesar, and the [things] of God to God."

**26.** And they were not able to seize-upon his word before the people, and having marveled at his answer, they were-silent.

**27.** And some of the Sadducees, having come-to [him] — those saying [there] is no resurrection — questioned him,

**28.** saying, "Teacher, Moses wrote for us, if a [man's] brother should die, having a wife, and this [one] should be childless, that his brother should take the wife and should raise-up seed for his brother.

**29.** There were therefore seven brothers; and the first, having taken a wife, died childless.

**30.** And the second,

**31.** and the third took her; and likewise also the seven — they did not leave-behind children, and they died.

**32.** Afterward the woman also died.

**33.** The woman therefore, in the resurrection, of which of them does she become wife? For the seven had her [as] wife."

**34.** And Jesus said to them, "The sons of this age marry and are given-in-marriage,

**35.** but those having been considered-worthy to attain that age and the resurrection, the [one] from [the] dead, neither marry nor are given-in-marriage;

**36.** for neither are they able to die any-longer, for they are equal-to-angels, and are sons of God, being sons of the resurrection.

**37.** But that the dead are raised, even Moses disclosed at the bush, as he says, '[The] Lord, the God of Abraham and [the] God of Isaac and [the] God of Jacob.'

**38.** But he is not God of [the] dead, but of [the] living; for all are living to him."

**39.** And some of the scribes, having answered, said, "Teacher, you spoke well."

**40.** For they no longer dared to question him [about] anything.

**41.** And he said to them, "How do they say the Christ is [the] son of David?

**42.** For David himself says in [the] book of Psalms, '[The] Lord said to my Lord, "Sit at my right-[hand],

**43.** until I should place your enemies [as] a footstool of your feet."'

**44.** David therefore calls him Lord, and how is he his son?"

**45.** And [while] all the people [were] hearing, he said to his disciples,

**46.** "Beware of the scribes, those wanting to walk in long-robes, and loving greetings in the market-places, and first-seats in the synagogues, and first-couches at the banquets,

**47.** who devour the houses of the widows, and for-a-pretense pray [at] length. These will receive more-abundant judgment."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **20:1 — "stood-upon [him]" (επεστησαν):** Sinaiticus reads και εγενετο εν μια των ημερων διδασκοντος αυτου τον λαον εν τω ιερω και ευαγγελιζομενου επεστησαν οι αρχιερεις και οι γραμματεις συν τοις πρεσβυτεροις. The verb εφιστημι ("to stand-upon/to come-upon suddenly") in the aorist (επεστησαν) conveys sudden, aggressive confrontation — they did not approach but descended upon him. Luke uses this verb for hostile arrivals (cf. 2:9 where the angel "stood-upon" the shepherds causing terror; Acts 4:1; 6:12). The delegation is a formal coalition: chief-priests (the temple establishment), scribes (the legal experts), and elders (the lay aristocracy) — the same three groups that form the Sanhedrin. Their arrival interrupts a double activity: teaching (διδασκοντος) and proclaiming-the-good-news (ευαγγελιζομενου). The verb ευαγγελιζομαι is distinctively Lukan (used 10 times in the Gospel, 15 times in Acts) and denotes the proclamation of salvation. The authorities interrupt not merely a lecture but the announcement of God's kingdom. The temporal marker εν μια των ημερων ("on one of the days") is deliberately vague — Luke does not specify which day, collapsing Jesus' final temple ministry into a series of confrontations whose specific chronology matters less than their theological escalation.

2. **20:2 — "by what authority" (εν ποια εξουσια):** Sinaiticus reads ειπον ημιν εν ποια εξουσια ταυτα ποιεις η τις εστιν ο δους σοι την εξουσιαν ταυτην. The question has two prongs: (1) "by what kind of authority" (εν ποια εξουσια) — what is the nature and source of your authority? and (2) "who is the one having given you this authority?" (τις εστιν ο δους σοι) — who authorized you? The word εξουσια appears three times in two verses, hammering the point: the temple leadership claims sole jurisdiction over what happens in the temple precincts. The demonstrative ταυτα ("these [things]") refers to both the teaching and the temple-cleansing of 19:45-46 — Jesus had disrupted the commercial operations they oversaw. The participle ο δους ("the [one] having given") is aorist, pointing to a specific moment of authorization. They demand Jesus name his ordaining authority — a trap, since any answer could be used against him.

3. **20:4-7 — The baptism of John:** Sinaiticus reads το βαπτισμα Ιωανου εξ ουρανου ην η εξ ανθρωπων. Jesus' counter-question is not evasion but exposure: the leaders' inability to answer reveals that their question about authority was never sincere. The verb συνελογισαντο ("they reasoned-together") indicates private, calculated deliberation — they are computing political consequences, not seeking truth. Their fear of the people — πας ο λαος καταλιθασει ημας ("all the people will stone us") — reveals that public opinion, not theological conviction, governs their positions. The verb καταλιθαζω ("to stone completely," with the intensifying prefix κατα-) suggests violent mob justice. The perfect passive participle πεπεισμενος ("having been persuaded") indicates the people's settled, enduring conviction that John was a prophet. The leaders' answer — μη ειδεναι ποθεν ("not to know from-where") — is a deliberate lie: they do have an opinion, but fear prevents them from stating it. Jesus' refusal to answer — ουδε εγω λεγω υμιν ("neither do I tell you") — is not a retreat but a judgment: those who will not answer honest questions have forfeited the right to receive answers.

4. **20:9 — "planted a vineyard" (εφυτευσεν αμπελωνα):** Sinaiticus reads ανθρωπος εφυτευσεν αμπελωνα και εξεδετο αυτον γεωργοις και απεδημησεν χρονους ικανους. The parable echoes Isaiah 5:1-7 (the Song of the Vineyard), where the vineyard is explicitly identified as "the house of Israel" (Isaiah 5:7). Every hearer would have recognized the allusion. The verb εκδιδωμι ("to let-out/to lease") indicates a tenant-farming arrangement common in first-century Palestine: absentee landowners leased land to farmers who paid rent in produce. The phrase χρονους ικανους ("considerable times/a long time") — unique to Luke's version — emphasizes the owner's patience and the tenants' long tenure. The word γεωργος ("tenant-farmer," literally "earth-worker," from γη "earth" + εργον "work") carries no negative connotation in itself; the villainy lies in the tenants' actions, not their occupation.

5. **20:10-12 — The three servants sent:** Sinaiticus records an escalating pattern of violence. The first servant: δειραντες αυτον εξαπεστειλαν κενον ("having beaten him, they sent [him] away empty") — δερω means "to skin/to flay," hence "to beat severely." The second: κακεινον δειραντες και ατιμασαντες εξαπεστειλαν κενον ("that [one] also, having beaten and having dishonored, they sent [him] away empty") — the addition of ατιμαζω ("to dishonor/to treat shamefully") marks escalation. The third: και τουτον τραυματισαντες εξεβαλον ("this [one] also, having wounded, they cast-out") — τραυματιζω ("to wound") indicates physical injury, and εκβαλλω ("to cast-out") replaces the earlier "sent away," suggesting increasingly violent expulsion. Luke's three-servant sequence is more compressed than Mark's (12:1-5), which lists multiple servants. Each servant represents a prophet sent to Israel, and the escalating violence mirrors Israel's historical treatment of its prophets (cf. Nehemiah 9:26; Jeremiah 7:25-26; 2 Chronicles 36:15-16).

6. **20:13 — "my beloved son" (τον υιον μου τον αγαπητον):** Sinaiticus reads τι ποιησω πεμψω τον υιον μου τον αγαπητον ισως τουτον εντραπησονται. The owner's deliberation — τι ποιησω ("what shall I do?") — echoes the rich fool's soliloquy in 12:17 (the same phrase), but with opposite intent: the fool hoards for himself while the vineyard owner risks everything to reclaim what is his. The phrase τον υιον μου τον αγαπητον ("my beloved son") uses the same words spoken by God at Jesus' baptism (3:22: συ ει ο υιος μου ο αγαπητος) and transfiguration (9:35: ουτος εστιν ο υιος μου ο εκλελεγμενος — though Sinaiticus there reads εκλελεγμενος "chosen" rather than αγαπητος "beloved"). The parable is transparent allegory: the "beloved son" is Jesus himself. The word ισως ("perhaps") introduces genuine uncertainty — the owner does not guarantee the tenants will respect his son, only hopes they might. This "perhaps" is theologically startling: God sends his Son knowing rejection is possible, even probable.

7. **20:14-15 — "This is the heir; let us kill him":** Sinaiticus reads ιδοντες δε αυτον οι γεωργοι διελογιζοντο προς αλληλους λεγοντες ουτος εστιν ο κληρονομος αποκτεινωμεν αυτον ινα ημων γενηται η κληρονομια. The tenants' reasoning is calculated murder motivated by greed: they recognize the heir (ο κληρονομος) and conclude that killing him will transfer the inheritance to them. The subjunctive αποκτεινωμεν ("let us kill") is hortatory — they incite one another. The legal assumption may reflect a principle where unclaimed property could revert to occupants, but the logic is absurd: killing the owner's son will not legitimize the tenants' claim — it will provoke the owner's wrath. The detail και εκβαλοντες αυτον εξω του αμπελωνος απεκτειναν ("having cast him outside the vineyard, they killed [him]") — casting him outside before killing — corresponds to Jesus' crucifixion outside the walls of Jerusalem (Hebrews 13:12). Luke and Matthew place the casting-out before the killing (unlike Mark 12:8 where they kill first, then cast out), aligning the parable more precisely with the geographical fact of Golgotha's location outside the city.

8. **20:16 — "May-it-not-be!" (μη γενοιτο):** Sinaiticus reads ελευσεται και απολεσει τους γεωργους τουτους και δωσει τον αμπελωνα αλλοις· ακουσαντες δε ειπαν μη γενοιτο. The crowd's response μη γενοιτο ("may-it-not-be!") — the optative of γινομαι with the negative μη — is an expression of horrified protest, the strongest Greek negative wish. Paul uses this phrase repeatedly in Romans (3:4, 6, 31; 6:2, 15; 7:7, 13; 9:14; 11:1, 11). The crowd understands the parable's implications: the vineyard (Israel) will be given to "others" (αλλοις) — to those outside the current tenancy. Their cry is not disagreement with the owner's justice but dismay at what this means for them. The verb απολεσει ("he will destroy") is the same root as απολλυμι — total destruction, not mere discipline.

9. **20:17 — "The stone which the builders rejected" (λιθον ον απεδοκιμασαν οι οικοδομουντες):** Sinaiticus reads ο δε εμβλεψας αυτοις ειπεν τι ουν εστιν το γεγραμμενον τουτο λιθον ον απεδοκιμασαν οι οικοδομουντες ουτος εγενηθη εις κεφαλην γωνιας. Jesus quotes Psalm 118:22 (LXX 117:22). The verb εμβλεπω ("to look-at intently/to gaze upon") indicates a penetrating, direct look — he fixes his eyes on them before delivering the scriptural verdict. The verb αποδοκιμαζω ("to reject after testing/to declare unfit") implies the builders examined the stone and deliberately discarded it — this is not accidental oversight but professional judgment. The κεφαλη γωνιας ("head of [the] corner") is the cornerstone or capstone — the stone that holds the entire structure together, the most important stone in the building. The rejected stone becomes the most essential stone: the reversal is total. The perfect passive γεγραμμενον ("having been written") emphasizes the permanent, authoritative character of Scripture — what has been written stands written.

10. **20:18 — "will be broken-to-pieces...will crush" (συνθλασθησεται...λικμησει):** Sinaiticus reads πας ο πεσων επ εκεινον τον λιθον συνθλασθησεται εφ ον δ αν πεση λικμησει αυτον. This verse alludes to Isaiah 8:14-15 (the stone of stumbling) and Daniel 2:34-35, 44 (the stone that crushes the statue). Two kinds of encounter with the stone are described: (1) falling upon it — συνθλαω ("to break-together/to shatter") describes the breaking of bones or pottery; (2) the stone falling upon someone — λικμαω ("to winnow/to crush to dust") is more devastating, reducing the victim to chaff. The first is collision with the stone from above; the second is the stone's descent in judgment. Both are catastrophic, but the second is annihilating. The stone cannot be ignored: one either builds upon it or is destroyed by it.

11. **20:19-20 — Spies sent:** Sinaiticus reads και εζητησαν οι γραμματεις και οι αρχιερεις επιβαλειν επ αυτον τας χειρας εν αυτη τη ωρα και εφοβηθησαν τον λαον εγνωσαν γαρ οτι προς αυτους ειπεν την παραβολην ταυτην. The phrase εν αυτη τη ωρα ("in that [very] hour") conveys immediacy — they wanted to arrest him on the spot. The verb εφοβηθησαν ("they feared") is aorist passive, indicating fear that seized them. Then in v. 20: παρατηρησαντες απεστειλαν εγκαθετους υποκρινομενους εαυτους δικαιους ειναι. The word εγκαθετος ("spy/planted agent," from εγκαθιημι, "to send-in secretly") appears only here in the NT — these are agents provocateurs, secretly planted men. The participle υποκρινομενους ("pretending/playing a role") is from υποκρινομαι, whence "hypocrite" — they are literally performing a role, acting the part of sincere questioners. Their purpose: to seize-upon a word (επιλαβωνται αυτου λογου) and hand him over to the ηγεμων ("governor") — the Roman prefect, Pontius Pilate. The transfer from religious to political jurisdiction signals a new phase in the plot against Jesus.

12. **20:21-22 — "Is it lawful to give tribute to Caesar?":** Sinaiticus reads διδασκαλε οιδαμεν οτι ορθως λεγεις και διδασκεις και ου λαμβανεις προσωπον αλλ επ αληθειας την οδον του θεου διδασκεις· εξεστιν ημας Καισαρι φορον δουναι η ου. The flattery is elaborate and calculated: ορθως ("rightly"), ου λαμβανεις προσωπον ("you do not receive [the] face" — a Hebraism for showing partiality, from Hebrew נָשָׂא פָנִים, nasa panim), and επ αληθειας ("upon truth"). The word φορος ("tribute") is specifically the Roman poll-tax — distinct from τελος (customs duties) or other taxes. This was the most hated tax: a direct payment to the occupying power, symbolizing subjugation. The trap is precise: if Jesus says "yes," he alienates the people who despise Roman taxation; if he says "no," he can be reported to Pilate as a revolutionary refusing to acknowledge Roman authority. The question εξεστιν ("is it lawful?") frames it as a Torah question: does God's Law permit paying tribute to a pagan emperor?

13. **20:23-25 — "Give-back the [things] of Caesar to Caesar" (αποδοτε τα Καισαρος Καισαρι):** Sinaiticus reads κατανοησας δε αυτων την πανουργιαν ειπεν προς αυτους δειξατε μοι δηναριον· τινος εχει εικονα και επιγραφην· οι δε ειπαν Καισαρος· ο δε ειπεν προς αυτους τοινυν αποδοτε τα Καισαρος Καισαρι και τα του θεου τω θεω. The word πανουργια ("craftiness/cunning," from πας "all" + εργον "work" — "ready to do anything") exposes their intent. Jesus requests a δηναριον (denarius) — he does not carry one himself. The coin bears Caesar's εικων ("image") — the same word used in Genesis 1:26-27 (LXX) for humanity made in God's "image." The verb αποδοτε ("give-back/render") is not merely "give" (δοτε) but "give-back" — return what already belongs to its owner. The answer transcends the trap by establishing two legitimate but distinct spheres of obligation: Caesar's coin bears Caesar's image and may be returned to him; but the human person bears God's image and belongs to God. The implication is devastating to the questioners: they are so preoccupied with Caesar's coin that they have forgotten whose image they themselves bear.

14. **20:27-33 — The Sadducees' question:** Sinaiticus reads προσελθοντες δε τινες των Σαδδουκαιων οι αντιλεγοντες αναστασιν μη ειναι. The participial phrase οι αντιλεγοντες αναστασιν μη ειναι ("those saying [there] is no resurrection") defines the Sadducees doctrinally — Luke explains for his Gentile audience what Sadducees believe (cf. Acts 23:8). The verb αντιλεγω ("to speak-against/to contradict") indicates active denial, not mere doubt. Their question is based on the levirate marriage law of Deuteronomy 25:5-6 — the obligation of a brother to marry his deceased brother's childless widow to raise up offspring in the dead brother's name. By constructing an extreme scenario (seven brothers all dying childless after marrying the same woman), they intend to show that resurrection leads to absurdity: whose wife would she be? The question assumes resurrection means mere resuscitation into the same social structures — an assumption Jesus will demolish. The number seven suggests they are constructing a deliberate reductio ad absurdum.

15. **20:34-36 — "equal-to-angels" (ισαγγελοι):** Sinaiticus reads οι δε καταξιωθεντες του αιωνος εκεινου τυχειν και της αναστασεως της εκ νεκρων ουτε γαμουσιν ουτε γαμιζονται ουτε γαρ αποθανειν ετι δυνανται ισαγγελοι γαρ εισιν και υιοι εισιν θεου της αναστασεως υιοι οντες. Jesus distinguishes two ages: ο αιων ουτος ("this age") where marriage is necessary because death requires procreation for continuity, and ο αιων εκεινος ("that age") where death is abolished and marriage is therefore unnecessary. The word ισαγγελοι ("equal-to-angels") is a hapax legomenon in the NT — a compound adjective (ισος "equal" + αγγελος "angel") found only here. It does not say the resurrected become angels but that they share the angels' deathlessness. The phrase υιοι θεου ("sons of God") is predicated on being υιοι της αναστασεως ("sons of the resurrection") — resurrection is not merely something that happens to them but something that defines their identity. The verb καταξιοω ("to consider-worthy/to deem-worthy") implies divine selection — not all automatically attain that age, but those whom God considers worthy.

16. **20:37-38 — "even Moses disclosed" (και Μωυσης εμηνυσεν):** Sinaiticus reads οτι δε εγειρονται οι νεκροι και Μωυσης εμηνυσεν επι της βατου ως λεγει κυριον τον θεον Αβρααμ και θεον Ισαακ και θεον Ιακωβ. Jesus strategically appeals to Moses — the only authority the Sadducees accepted — and specifically to the burning bush passage (Exodus 3:6, 15). The verb μηνυω ("to disclose/to reveal/to make known") implies that Moses gave evidence for resurrection even though the Torah does not explicitly teach it. The argument is subtle: God identifies himself as "the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob" — present tense, not "the God who once was associated with Abraham." If the patriarchs have ceased to exist, God would not identify himself by relationship to non-entities. The conclusion — θεος δε ουκ εστιν νεκρων αλλα ζωντων παντες γαρ αυτω ζωσιν ("but he is not God of [the] dead, but of [the] living; for all are living to him") — asserts that from God's perspective, the patriarchs are alive now. The dative αυτω ("to him") is crucial: they live "to him" / "in his sight" — their continued existence is grounded in God's relationship to them, not in any inherent immortality.

17. **20:39-40 — "You spoke well" (καλως ειπας):** Sinaiticus reads αποκριθεντες δε τινες των γραμματεων ειπαν διδασκαλε καλως ειπας· ουκετι γαρ ετολμων επερωταν αυτον ουδεν. The scribes (who were Pharisees and believed in resurrection) commend Jesus for silencing the Sadducees — their theological rivals. The adverb καλως ("well/beautifully") is genuine praise. But the narrator's comment — ουκετι γαρ ετολμων ("for they no longer dared") — applies to all questioners, including the scribes. Jesus has defeated every challenge: the authority question (vv. 1-8), the tribute trap (vv. 20-26), and the resurrection riddle (vv. 27-38). No one dares ask anything more. The verb τολμαω ("to dare/to have courage") indicates that further questioning would require a courage they no longer possess.

18. **20:41-44 — "How do they say the Christ is [the] son of David?":** Sinaiticus reads ειπεν δε προς αυτους πως λεγουσιν τον Χριστον ειναι Δαυειδ υιον αυτος γαρ Δαυειδ λεγει εν βιβλω ψαλμων ειπεν κυριος τω κυριω μου καθου εκ δεξιων μου εως αν θω τους εχθρους σου υποποδιον των ποδων σου. Having silenced all questioners, Jesus now poses his own question. He quotes Psalm 110:1 (LXX 109:1) — ειπεν κυριος τω κυριω μου — "the Lord (YHWH) said to my Lord (the Messiah)." The argument: if David, writing under inspiration, calls the Messiah "my Lord" (κυριος μου), how can the Messiah be merely David's descendant ("son")? A father does not call his own son "my Lord." The question does not deny Davidic descent but transcends it: the Messiah is David's son by lineage but David's Lord by nature. The verb καθου ("sit," from καθημαι) is an imperative — God commands the Messiah to sit at his right hand, the position of supreme authority and co-regency. The phrase υποποδιον των ποδων σου ("a footstool of your feet") is ancient Near Eastern imagery: victorious kings placed their feet on the necks of conquered enemies. The Messiah's enemies will be made his footstool — total subjugation.

19. **20:45-47 — Warning about the scribes:** Sinaiticus reads ακουοντος δε παντος του λαου ειπεν τοις μαθηταις αυτου προσεχετε απο των γραμματεων των θελοντων περιπατειν εν στολαις και φιλουντων ασπασμους εν ταις αγοραις και πρωτοκαθεδριας εν ταις συναγωγαις και πρωτοκλισιας εν τοις δειπνοις οι κατεσθιουσιν τας οικιας των χηρων και προφασει μακρα προσευχονται ουτοι λημψονται περισσοτερον κριμα. The warning is spoken to the disciples but deliberately within earshot of "all the people" (παντος του λαου). The στολη ("long-robe") was the distinctive garment of the learned — an ankle-length robe that signaled scholarly status and made manual labor impossible. The compound nouns πρωτοκαθεδρια ("first-seat," from πρωτος "first" + καθεδρα "seat/chair") and πρωτοκλισια ("first-couch," from πρωτος + κλισις "reclining-place") indicate the most honorable positions at synagogue worship and banquets respectively. The verb κατεσθιω ("to devour/to eat-down") — intensified form of εσθιω ("to eat") — is violently metaphorical: they consume widows' estates as one devours food. The word προφασις ("pretense/pretext") indicates their lengthy prayers are performance, not devotion. The comparative περισσοτερον κριμα ("more-abundant judgment") indicates that religious leaders who exploit the vulnerable while wearing the mask of piety will receive heavier condemnation than ordinary sinners — their hypocrisy compounds their injustice.

20. **20:1-47 — The structure of Luke 20:** The chapter is organized as a series of five confrontations in the temple, escalating in theological depth: (1) The question of authority (vv. 1-8) — Jesus refuses to answer those who refuse to answer honestly; (2) The parable of the wicked tenants (vv. 9-19) — Jesus narrates salvation history as vineyard violence, predicting his own death and the transfer of God's vineyard; (3) The tribute to Caesar (vv. 20-26) — Jesus distinguishes divine and imperial claims without subordinating either; (4) The resurrection debate (vv. 27-40) — Jesus establishes the reality of resurrection from the Torah itself; (5) David's Lord (vv. 41-44) — Jesus poses the question no one can answer, establishing that the Messiah transcends Davidic categories. The chapter concludes with the warning against the scribes (vv. 45-47), which transitions directly into the widow's offering (21:1-4). The movement is from external challenge (others questioning Jesus) to internal initiative (Jesus questioning them and then warning about them). By the end of the chapter, all opposition has been silenced — not persuaded, but silenced — and Jesus holds unchallenged authority in the temple, the very space whose custodians challenged his right to be there.
